- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- less-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- react-doctor: Diagnose and fix React codebase health issues. Use when reviewing React code, fixing performance problems, auditing security, or improving code quality.
- ralph: Convert PRDs to prd.json format for the Ralph autonomous agent system. Use when you have an existing PRD and need to convert it to Ralph's JSON format. Triggers on: convert this prd, turn this into ralph format, create prd.json from this, ralph json.
- adversarial-review: Adversarial code review using the opposite model. Spawns 1–3 reviewers on the opposing model (Claude spawns Codex, Codex spawns Claude) to challenge work from distinct critical lenses. Triggers: "adversarial review".
- receiving-code-review: Use when receiving code review feedback, before implementing suggestions, especially if feedback seems unclear or technically questionable - requires technical rigor and verification, not performative agreement or blind implementation
- tdd: Test-driven development with red-green-refactor loop. Use when user wants to build features or fix bugs using TDD, mentions "red-green-refactor", wants integration tests, or asks for test-first development.
- grill-me: Interview the user relentlessly about a plan or design until reaching shared understanding, resolving each branch of the decision tree. Use when user wants to stress-test a plan, get grilled on their design, or mentions "grill me".
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

Your branch has diverged from `origin/feat/backend-testing-suite`:
- **14 commits** ahead locally
- **8 commits** on the remote you don't have

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- When branches diverge, `git pull` isn't a safe default — it either creates a merge commit or rebases depending on your git config (`pull.rebase`). Each path rewrites history differently.
- With 14 local commits at stake, a bad pull could clutter history with a merge bubble or (with rebase) force you to resolve conflicts commit-by-commit across 14 replays.
- Safer workflow: `git fetch` first, inspect the remote commits with `git log HEAD..origin/...`, then choose merge vs rebase deliberately.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Before pulling, how would you like to integrate?
1. **Merge** (`git pull --no-rebase`) — creates a merge commit, preserves both histories
2. **Rebase** (`git pull --rebase`) — replays your 14 commits on top of the 8 remote ones (linear, but may conflict repeatedly)
3. **Fetch only** — I pull down `origin/feat/backend-testing-suite` without integrating, so you can inspect first

Which do you want?

Rebase hit conflicts on commit `e2358e6` ("feat: add guided student walkthrough"). Git auto-resolved `Nav.tsx`, `root.tsx`, and `tsconfig.json` via `rerere` (recorded prior resolutions), but `package.json` needs manual intervention.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- `git rerere` ("reuse recorded resolution") is enabled here — that's why Nav.tsx, root.tsx, and tsconfig.json resolved automatically. Git remembered how you fixed those conflicts before and replayed the resolution.
- The "skipped previously applied commit" lines mean 5 of your local commits are already in the remote — git detected them by patch-id and dropped them to avoid duplicates. That's why you're rebasing 9 commits instead of 14.
- `package.json` conflicts almost always stem from dependency version bumps on both sides. These need human judgment about which version to keep.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

content.text:
Here's the conflict breakdown. Auto-merge kept **both** `"test"` keys (lines 9 and 12) because git merged non-adjacent lines — but duplicate JSON keys is invalid.

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- Git's line-based 3-way merge doesn't understand JSON semantics — it happily produces a file with duplicate keys if the two `"test"` entries land on different lines. This is a classic "semantic conflict without textual conflict."
- The branch you're on is `feat/backend-testing-suite`, explicitly built around vitest. The incoming commit `e2358e6` is for the student walkthrough feature and uses `bun test`. The branch purpose settles it: keep vitest.
- `bun-types` is a dev dep only useful for `bun test`'s TypeScript declarations — without that script, it's dead weight.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

**Proposed resolution:**

| Line | HEAD (this branch) | e2358e6 (incoming) | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| `scripts.test` | `"vitest run"` | `"bun test"` | **Keep HEAD** (branch is vitest-based) |
| devDeps jsdom/knip/oxfmt/oxlint | ✓ present | — | **Keep HEAD** |
| devDeps bun-types | — | ✓ present | **Drop** (no bun test script) |

Want me to apply this resolution to `package.json` and continue the rebase? Or do you want `bun-types` kept / a different test script?

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- The pattern is clear: HEAD uses modern tooling (Vite 8, oxlint/oxfmt, knip, react-router 7.13.1, tsgo), incoming uses older (Vite 6, bun-types, vite-tsconfig-paths, 7.7.1). This branch is strictly newer in every dependency dimension.
- `add/add` conflicts happen when both branches independently added the same file path. Since this branch is named `feat/backend-testing-suite`, *its* test files are the canonical implementation — the incoming commit is a parallel attempt.
- `package-lock.json` conflicts should almost always be resolved by taking one side and running `npm install` after — manual merge of lockfiles is error-prone.
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

Let me look at the add/add test files to verify HEAD is the right choice.

=== .githooks/pre-commit (HEAD) ===
#!/bin/sh
# Pre-commit hook: lint, format, typecheck, and test ONLY what staged changes touch.
# Skip with: git commit --no-verify

set -e

# Collect staged files (added/modified, not deleted)
STAGED=$(git diff --cached --name-only --diff-filter=d)

if [ -z "$STAGED" ]; then
  echo "No staged files — skipping checks."
  exit 0
fi

# Categorize staged files
FE_FILES=$(echo "$STAGED" | grep -E '^(app|shared)/' || true)
BE_FILES=$(echo "$STAGED" | grep -E '^server/(src|test)/' || true)
ALL_SRC=$(echo "$STAGED" | grep -E '\.(ts|tsx|js|jsx)$' || true)
TS_FILES=$(echo "$STAGED" | grep -E '\.(ts|tsx)$' || true)

# ── Format check (only staged source files) ──────────────────────────
if [ -n "$ALL_SRC" ]; then
  echo "Checking formatting on $(echo "$ALL_SRC" | wc -l | tr -d ' ') staged files..."
  echo "$ALL_SRC" | xargs npx oxfmt --check --no-error-on-unmatched-pattern 2>&1
fi

# ── Lint (only staged source files) ──────────────────────────────────
if [ -n "$ALL_SRC" ]; then
  echo "Linting $(echo "$ALL_SRC" | wc -l | tr -d ' ') staged files..."
  echo "$ALL_SRC" | xargs npx oxlint --quiet 2>&1
fi

# ── Type check (runs tsgo when any .ts/.tsx file is staged) ──────────
# tsgo is project-wide but at ~0.7s it's faster than most lint runs
if [ -n "$TS_FILES" ]; then
  echo "Type-checking with tsgo..."
  npx tsgo --noEmit 2>&1
fi

# ── Backend tests (only if server/ files changed) ────────────────────
if [ -n "$BE_FILES" ]; then
  echo "Running backend tests affected by changes..."
  cd server && npx vitest run --changed HEAD --reporter=dot 2>&1
  cd ..
else
  echo "No backend changes — skipping backend tests."
fi

# ── Frontend tests (only if app/ files changed) ──────────────────────
if [ -n "$FE_FILES" ]; then
  echo "Running frontend tests affected by changes..."
  npx vitest run --changed HEAD --reporter=dot 2>&1
else
  echo "No frontend changes — skipping frontend tests."
fi

echo "All checks passed."
